Glossary About
Late Antiquity

Alexander of Aphrodisias

fl. c. 200 CE

The great Peripatetic commentator: a rigorously naturalistic reading of Aristotle’s De Anima.

Alexander of Aphrodisias is the clearest case in antiquity of someone trying not to harmonize Aristotle with Plato, but to recover and harden what he took to be Aristotle’s own position against rival schools. He was active in the late second and early third century CE. For the question of soul, the safest core is his own De anima (On the Soul) and De fato (On Fate). The transmitted Supplement or Mantissa is a collection of shorter pieces of uneven status, though many are closely connected with Alexander’s other work, and the separate De intellectu is textually disputed, so it should be handled more cautiously. What follows is therefore as primary-source-forward as the evidence allows, with the main weight on Alexander’s own surviving De anima and On Fate.

The shortest accurate thesis is this: Alexander radicalizes Aristotelian hylomorphism against both Platonic separability and Stoic corporealism. Soul, for him, is neither a body nor a self-moving substance nor a harmony nor a mixture. It is the form and entelechy of the organized living body. But he also makes the soul’s dependence on bodily constitution unusually explicit: the body and its qualitative mixture are causes of the soul’s coming-to-be, and what we call the soul’s activities are, for the most part, activities of the ensouled composite rather than of soul in isolation. At the level of intellect he then makes a second, equally characteristic move: the only truly imperishable intellect is the productive intellect “from outside,” which he treats as separate and bound up with the first cause, not as the ordinary personal soul. In ethics and action-theory he places what depends on us in rational assent and deliberation, and uses that to attack Stoic determinism.

I am taking your Plato and Aristotle reports as the baseline for comparison. Those reports present Plato’s soul as often self-moving, immortal, more separable from body, and internally conflictual in a way formalized by tripartition, while Aristotle’s soul is the form or first actuality of the living organized body, with psychology continuous with biology and with only a narrow, difficult opening around intellect. Alexander stands overwhelmingly nearer to Aristotle than to Plato, but he also sharpens Aristotle at exactly the points where later antiquity found him obscure.

1. What soul is: form, entelechy, and the living composite

Alexander’s official answer is unmistakably Aristotelian. Soul is the entelechy of a natural body. In his De anima he states that the soul is the entelechy of a natural body and argues that the living body is not any random body, but specifically the body whose life consists in nourishing itself and growing through itself. He also insists that the soul is the form of the living thing: when the soul is gone, the animal no longer remains what it previously was, just as a shaped tool without its form is no longer that tool in the relevant sense.

This hylomorphic point is not merely a slogan for Alexander. He spends time blocking two common misunderstandings. First, the soul is not an accident inhering in a pre-existing subject the way color inheres in a surface. He explicitly argues that the soul is not “in a substrate” in that sense, because then it would have to be able to occur in any body whatever, even simple bodies like fire, air, water, or earth. That, he says, is impossible. The soul’s own matter is the organized body, and that organized body is not organized before it has soul or after it has lost it. Second, because soul is form, Alexander also says that soul is incorporeal: every form, precisely as form, is incorporeal. This is one of the cleanest anti-Stoic moments in his psychology.

But “incorporeal” here does not mean “Platonic inmate temporarily trapped in matter.” Alexander’s whole point is that form and living body are reciprocally intelligible. The soul is not a detachable thing standing over against the body as one complete substance against another. The living being is the composite of body as subject or matter and soul as form. That is why he can say both that soul is incorporeal and that when soul is separated from body the animal ceases to be an animal. The incorporeality is formal, not a license for Phaedo-style self-subsistence.

This becomes still clearer when Alexander turns from ontology to activity. In one striking passage he says that what we call the “activities of the soul” are not activities of soul by itself, but of the living composite that has soul. Joy, anger, pain, and comparable states belong to the ensouled human being, not to soul in isolation. That is very close to Aristotle’s own procedure in De anima and the Parva Naturalia, and very far from any view on which most psychic life can be understood apart from the body that realizes it.

2. Against reduction: not pneuma, not harmony, not mere mixture

Alexander’s most revealing work often consists in polemic. He is especially concerned to prevent soul from being reduced either to bodily stuff or to a bodily ratio. He explicitly attacks the view that soul is pneuma. If soul were merely pneuma plus some added quality, then, he argues, what had changed would just be the quality of the pneuma; one would still not have explained why that pneuma had become soul. The target is transparently Stoic. Stoic school doctrine defines soul as breath or pneuma and treats it as corporeal; Alexander’s complaint is that this turns soul into a modified body rather than the form of a living body.

He also rejects the identification of soul with harmony or mixture. In the De anima he rehearses positions that make soul either a harmony, or a composition of bodies, or something generated from the ratio of a mixture. He explicitly names the Stoics as calling soul a kind of pneuma compounded from air and fire, mentions the Epicureans as likewise treating soul as composite, and says that “according to Plato too” the substance of soul comes from a composition of certain constituents “as he says in the Timaeus.” He refuses all of these if they mean that the soul simply is the mixture or the ratio.

What makes Alexander especially interesting, though, is that he does not simply bounce back from these rivals to a flatly anti-physiological doctrine. He says soul is not a disposition, affection, or mixture. Yet he immediately adds that it is a certain power and substance arising on the basis of those bodily conditions, and that the body and its mixture are causes of the soul’s initial generation. He even says, very forcefully, that souls do not first sculpt bodily forms; rather, different souls follow upon different qualitative constitutions of bodily parts. So Alexander rejects reduction, but he also intensifies psychophysical dependence. The soul is not the mixture; it is the form-power that comes to be on the basis of the mixture. That is one of his most distinctive positions.

This is the point at which Alexander most clearly departs from both Plato and the Stoics at once. Against Plato, he refuses to make the soul ontologically prior to embodiment in the strong way the Phaedo and Timaeus often do. Against the Stoics, he refuses to make soul one more body among bodies. The result is a strongly naturalized hylomorphism: form, not body; but form that comes-to-be only with a certain body.

3. One soul, ordered powers, and the common critical center

Alexander’s internal psychology is likewise Aristotelian in architecture, but more schematic and in some respects more explicit. He insists on one soul, not several souls. In a remarkable passage he argues that if the rational power existed apart from the lower powers as something separately installed in us, we would have to say that each human being had several souls and was, in effect, several animals. That he treats as absurd. The higher powers must therefore include the lower within one ordered psychic whole.

Accordingly, he lays out the soul as an ordered hierarchy of powers. The first is the nutritive, with growth and generation coupled to it; by this power the ensouled is first distinguished from the non-ensouled, and life in its most basic sense is self-nourishment and growth. Above that comes sensation, which is distinctive of animals and is defined in recognizably Aristotelian terms as receiving the forms of sensibles without their matter. Above that comes the impulsive or desiderative power; and, in the case of human beings, the rational or critical power. The important point is not just plurality but order: higher powers presuppose lower ones, and the one soul has levels rather than rival substances.

Alexander then adds something that matters enormously for later comparison. He says there is a common critical faculty, a koinon kritikon. Particular senses discriminate particular sensibles through their own organs, but the comparison of unlike sensibles and the more general acts of judging require one common source of discrimination. He explicitly argues that the difference between, say, white and sweet cannot be judged by completely separate faculties at different times; there must be one and the same discerner. From that same common critical source, he says, arise imagination, assent, apprehension, supposition, belief, knowledge, reasoning, and thought.

That move is important for two reasons. First, it is anti-Platonic in method: Alexander does not begin from a political partition of soul into semi-independent agents, but from the coordination of powers in one organism. Second, it shows that he is not untouched by later Hellenistic vocabulary. The inclusion of assent (synkatathesis) in the common critical economy gives his psychology a point of contact with Stoic moral psychology, even while his ontology remains anti-Stoic. He also posits a common impulsive or appetitive faculty, and says appetite includes desire, spirited impulse, and wish. In another passage he orders the sequence even more sharply: imagination, memory, and assent belong to the sensory-critical side; impulse is the practical terminus, and assent leads impulse, while imagination leads assent.

Alexander therefore preserves something like rational rule without accepting Platonic tripartition. The soul has powers, not factions. There is spiritedness and desire, but as forms of appetite within a single functional structure. There is a common critical source, but not a Stoic bodily pneuma. There is a ruling center, but it is the ruling center of one hylomorphic soul, not the commander of a soul-body dualism.

4. The hegemonikon and the heart

Alexander is also cardiocentric. He argues that the rational soul, together with the sensory, desiderative, and practical powers, must be located around the heart, and he concludes that the hegemonic or ruling part of the soul in animals is established there. He then distinguishes the hegemonic from the ministering or instrumental aspect, since acts of judging and acting require organs, but the ruling center itself is around the heart. He even rejects arguments that would move the ruling part to the head or brain.

This is one of the places where Alexander stands in an interesting relation to Aristotle and the Stoics at once. It is straight in line with Aristotelian cardiocentrism as your Aristotle report set it out, but it also converges with the Stoic location of the ruling part in the heart. So on the location of the psychic center Alexander and the Stoics overlap, even while they disagree sharply about the ontology of the soul that has that center.

5. Soul does not move itself

Here Alexander is at his most decisively anti-Platonic. He states that the impulsive and appetitive soul moves the animal “not by itself being moved.” Why? Because every entelechy, as entelechy, is itself unmoved in that strict sense. Since soul has already been shown to be entelechy, it is not properly said to move the body the way one distinct object moves another distinct object. The looser expression “the animal is moved by the soul” is permitted, but only because we also speak that way when something moves itself through an internal principle.

That is a direct refusal of the Platonic strategy that makes self-motion the defining mark of soul. In your Plato report, the Phaedrus and Laws were central precisely because they define soul through self-motion and priority to body. Alexander moves the other way. Soul is not the first self-moving principle that later happens to govern body; it is the form of the organized living thing, and its “motion” is the realized life of that thing.

6. Intellect: the most difficult and most Alexanderian part

The doctrine of intellect is where Alexander both follows Aristotle most closely and departs from everyone else most dramatically. His starting point is Aristotelian: if there is a material or receptive intellect in us, there must also be a productive intellect corresponding to it, just as in other natural processes there is matter and something productive of actuality. He says there is a material intellect and that there must therefore be a productive intellect which is the cause of the acquired state or actuality of the material intellect.

He then identifies that productive intellect in very elevated terms. The productive intellect is the most intelligible form, separate from matter. Alexander says that what is maximally and properly intelligible is cause of other things being intelligible, and he describes the productive intellect as the first cause, the cause and principle of being for all other things, separate, impassive, and unmixed. He even says that this is what Aristotle indicated by the first cause, and that this alone is intellect in the strictest sense. That is the heart of the Alexanderian reading: the productive intellect is not just a feature of our personal psychology, but a separate, higher principle.

From there Alexander draws the conclusion that only this higher actuality is imperishable in the strict sense. In one crucial passage he says that when what is thought is by nature such as it is thought and is imperishable, then, even when separated from being thought, it remains imperishable; and so the intellect that thought it is imperishable, but not the underlying or material intellect, which perishes together with the soul whose power it is. The imperishable intellect is the intellect “from outside” coming to be in us. This is one of the starkest ancient denials that ordinary human intellect as such is immortal.

So Alexander’s position is not Platonic immortality of the personal soul. Nor is it quite Aristotle’s own famously compressed and ambiguous presentation. It is a severe externalization of what is immortal. The personal, embodied intellective capacity belongs to the soul and perishes with it; the separate productive intellect is imperishable, but precisely because it is not simply “ours” in the ordinary sense. This is why later commentators, like Themistius, reacted so strongly against Alexander’s reading and tried to make the active intellect more fully a part of the human soul.

A textual caution matters here. The separate De intellectu transmitted under Alexander’s name is disputed, and some of the later tradition’s sharpest formulations come from that contested dossier. But the basic structure I have just described does not depend on that disputed text; it is already visible in Alexander’s own De anima.

7. Deliberation, rational assent, and what depends on us

If De anima gives Alexander’s ontology and psychology, On Fate gives the ethical and practical side of the same mind. The governing idea is that deliberation would be pointless unless some future actions were genuinely up to us. Alexander says we deliberate only about future things done by us; we do not deliberate about eternal things, necessary things, or things that depend entirely on other people. If we deliberate where no advantage can come from deliberating, then deliberation is vain. Since we do in fact deliberate about future actions of our own, deliberation must make a difference to what happens.

He puts the causal point with equal force. Not every event, he says, has its cause outside itself. For the things that depend on us, the cause is from us. Human being is the beginning and cause of the actions that come about through him; to be human is precisely to have the beginnings of acting in oneself in this way. That is his answer to the determinist complaint that variation in action would introduce motion without a cause: there is a cause, namely the agent.

Alexander then locates this domain of agency in rational assent. In the Mantissa material on what depends on us, he says quite flatly that what depends on us is located in rational assent. Non-rational animals assent too, but they simply follow sensory appearances. Human beings, by contrast, judge appearances through reason; and when, after deliberation, something no longer seems as it first appeared, they can withhold assent. In another passage he sharpens the distinction: not everything that “appears” is a mere appearance. Some things appear through reason and reasoning, and the person who assents on the basis of reasoning in deliberation is himself cause to himself of the assent.

This part of Alexander is worth stressing because it shows that his theory of soul is not only biological. Rational soul is what makes a specifically human mode of causation possible. We do not merely react to impressions; we can interrogate them, reason about them, and either assent or not assent. That is his Aristotelian alternative both to Platonic mythic psychodrama and to Stoic universal causal necessitation.

He is not naive about character, though. Alexander acknowledges that natural endowment, habits, and education powerfully shape what kind of people we become, and he says this creates a genuine puzzle for responsibility. His solution is not to deny the force of nature and habit, but to locate responsibility at the level of acquisition. Virtues and vices depend on us because, before we acquired them, it was in our power not to acquire them. Once a stable state is formed, the opposite may no longer be equally available; but praise and blame remain appropriate because we made ourselves the kind of people we became.

8. Fate against the Stoics

Alexander’s anti-Stoic polemic comes to a head in his treatment of fate. The Stoics define fate as an endless chain of causation. Alexander knows that definition and rejects it. He argues that not everything comes to be of necessity; what depends on us and what happens by luck show that contingency belongs within the domain of becoming. Choice and luck interrupt the continuity of what the Stoics imagine as an unbreakable sequence. Fate therefore cannot be absolute necessity or a chain that one cannot go against.

This does not mean that Alexander abolishes order. He is willing to say that what occurs “according to fate” occurs according to an order and sequence. But he wants that order to be compatible with contingency, choice, and luck. The difference from Stoicism is not that Alexander loves chaos and the Stoics love order. The difference is that the Stoics turn order into exhaustive necessitation, whereas Alexander tries to preserve order without making deliberation, choice, and responsibility illusory.

9. Compared with Plato

The deepest similarity between Alexander and Plato is ethical rather than ontological. Both agree that the soul is what matters most in a human being, that reason should rule, and that it is a philosophical mistake to reduce soul to a merely bodily by-product. There is also one very specific point of contact: Plato in the Phaedo rejects the harmony-theory of soul, and Alexander does too. But Alexander’s reasons and his destination are different. He rejects the harmony-model in order to defend hylomorphic form; Plato rejects it in order to defend the soul’s priority and immortality.

The differences are larger. First, Alexander rejects the defining Platonic claim that soul is self-moving. For Plato, as your report showed, self-motion is one of the deepest marks of soul; for Alexander, soul as entelechy is not self-moving in that strict sense at all. Second, he rejects the robust personal immortality central to the Phaedo and to the mythic endings of the Republic. In Alexander, the ordinary human soul belongs to the living composite and ceases with it; imperishability belongs only to the separate productive intellect from outside. Third, he refuses Plato’s most famous internal model. Where the Republic emphasizes rational, spirited, and appetitive parts in tension, Alexander insists on one soul with ordered powers, and even says that otherwise we would absurdly have several souls and several animals in one human being.

He also directly resists the Timaeus. Alexander says that “according to Plato” the substance of soul is from a certain composition and mixture “as he says in the Timaeus.” He rejects that way of construing soul’s substance. And unlike Platonic recollection, Alexander’s route to thought runs upward from sensation, common judgment, imagination, memory, assent, and reasoning. Nothing in his psychology requires the soul to have seen intelligible realities before embodiment.

So Alexander’s relation to Plato is not simple hostility. He shares the anti-reductionist seriousness of care for soul. But he strips that seriousness of the Platonic metaphysic of self-moving, pre-existent, and personally immortal soul. In his hands, soul becomes more immanent, more organic, and more tightly bound to embodiment than Plato would allow.

10. Compared with Aristotle

Here the continuity is overwhelming. Alexander’s official doctrine is almost a sustained elaboration of what your Aristotle report identified as central to Aristotle himself: soul as form or first actuality of the organized living body, psychology continuous with biology, the hierarchy of powers, sensation as receiving form without matter, common discrimination among sensibles, the embodied character of most psychic states, and the centrality of deliberation and choice in human action. Even his cardiocentrism and his insistence that the emotions belong to the ensouled composite rather than to soul alone are straight Aristotelian.

But Alexander is not merely repeating Aristotle. He sharpens at least two things. First, he makes psychophysical dependence much more explicit. Aristotle says soul is the form of the living body; Alexander adds, with unusual bluntness, that body and bodily mixture are causes of soul’s initial generation, and that the differences of soul track differences in bodily constitution. This gives Alexander’s hylomorphism a more pointed physiological edge than Aristotle’s extant formulations usually display. Second, Alexander resolves Aristotle’s difficult doctrine of active intellect in a very determinate direction: the productive intellect is not simply a mysterious aspect of us, but the separate first cause, while the intellect that belongs to the human soul perishes with it. Aristotle’s own text, as your report stressed, is far more compressed and less explicit at exactly this point.

So if one wants a formula, Alexander is not less Aristotelian than Aristotle, but more doctrinally exposed. He converts Aristotle’s compressed psychology into a school position sharp enough to fight Platonists and Stoics. That is why he matters so much historically.

11. Compared with the Stoics

Alexander is both nearer to the Stoics than a superficial summary would suggest, and farther from them than almost anyone else in antiquity. He is nearer in three respects. He uses the language of assent and makes rational assent central to what depends on us. He retains a hegemonic center of the soul and locates it around the heart. And like later Stoics such as Epictetus, he treats agency as turning on what happens in judgment and choice rather than on external events.

Yet the differences are fundamental. Stoic school doctrine says soul is corporeal warm breath or pneuma, and Seneca still says flatly that the soul is corporeal. Alexander explicitly attacks that view: soul is not pneuma, and because it is form it is incorporeal. The Stoics define fate as an endless chain of causation; Alexander rejects that definition and argues that choice and luck break the supposed continuity. The Stoics explain human action within a fully deterministic cosmos while still giving assent a functional role; Alexander makes rational assent the very site of what is genuinely up to us and uses that to deny universal necessitation.

There is also a subtler contrast. Stoic psychology tends to treat the soul as one corporeal ruling structure with functions diffused through it; Alexander also emphasizes unity and a ruling center, but his unifying principle is hylomorphic form, not bodily tension. And whereas Stoic ethics is built around the discipline of appearances and assent inside a providential chain of causes, Alexander’s action-theory preserves a thicker sense in which the human being is a beginning and cause of action from himself. In that respect he stands closer to Aristotle’s theory of deliberation and choice than to Stoic compatibilism, even though he has clearly absorbed some Stoic vocabulary.

Conclusion

Alexander’s doctrine of soul can be summed up this way. Soul is the incorporeal form and entelechy of the organized living body. It is not a body, not pneuma, not a harmony, not a bare mixture. But neither is it a Platonic self-moving traveler that can be understood first apart from embodiment. Its powers are ordered within one soul: nutritive, sensory, impulsive, rational. Its activities, for the most part, are activities of the living composite. Its ruling center is around the heart. Its distinctly human dignity lies in rational judgment, deliberation, and assent. And its most difficult point, intellect, is split between a perishable intellect that belongs to our soul and an imperishable productive intellect from outside, identified with the first cause.

That is why Alexander looks, in the end, like the great anti-Stoic and anti-Platonic Aristotelian. Against Plato, he de-transcendentalizes soul. Against the Stoics, he de-materializes it. Against both, he tries to make human agency real without breaking the order of nature. If Plato’s soul is too separable, and the Stoic soul too bodily, Alexander’s soul is the most rigorous ancient attempt to occupy the Aristotelian middle: embodied without being reducible, formal without being merely abstract, rational without being detached from animal life, and responsible without being swallowed by fate.

A strong next step would be a second pass focused only on Alexander’s intellect-doctrine, because that is the point where later Arabic, Latin, and Byzantine reception became especially intricate.
